elements.sent-2411-001: We introduce the relationship between congruent numbers and elliptic curves, and compute the conductor of the elliptic curve \( y^2 = x^3 - n^2 x \) associated with it.
elements.sent-2411-002: Furthermore, we prove that its \( L \)-series coefficient \( a_m = 0 \) when \( m \equiv 3 \mod 4 \).By using the invariants of the elliptic curve introduced above, we calculate Heegner points to quickly verify whether \( n \) is a congruent number.
elements.sent-2411-003: This problem dates back to ancient Greece, having been posed by Archimedes, and in modern number theory, it is deeply connected to elliptic curves, the Birch and Swinnerton-Dyer (BSD) conjecture, and the Goldfeld conjecture.
elements.sent-2411-004: Specifically, a positive integer $n$ is called a congruent number if and only if the Mordell-Weil group $E(\mathbb{Q )$ of the elliptic curve $E_n: y^2 = x^3 - n^2x$ is infinite, meaning that the elliptic curve has infinitely many rational points.}
elements.sent-2411-005: A square-free positive integer $n$ is called a congruent number if there exist rational numbers $a, b, c$ such that \begin{equation}a^2 + b^2 = c^2, \quad \frac{1}{2}ab = n.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-006: This problem can be studied through the properties of rational points on the elliptic curve $E_n$.
elements.sent-2411-007: The famous BSD conjecture suggests that when the derivative of the $L$-function $L(E, s)$ at $s = 1$ is non-zero, the Mordell-Weil group of $E_n$ is infinite, implying that $n$ is a congruent number.
elements.sent-2411-008: This is a key tool in determining whether $n$ is a congruent number.
elements.sent-2411-009: The proposition is as follows: Goldfeld Conjecture(Silverman, \cite{Silverman1986 ) If the elliptic curve $E_n$ satisfies certain conditions related to modular forms, then $L(E_n, 1) = 0$ if and only if $n$ is a congruent number.}
elements.sent-2411-010: Specifically, the core formula of the BSD conjecture is expressed as \begin{equation}rank(E(Q)) = ord_{s=1}L(E, s),\end{equation} which reveals a direct relationship between the order of vanishing of the $L$-function at $s = 1$ and the rank of the Mordell-Weil group of the elliptic curve.
elements.sent-2411-011: The Shafarevich-Tate group $(E/\mathbb{Q )$ is one of the key tools for studying the BSD conjecture.}
elements.sent-2411-012: It reflects whether the rational points on an elliptic curve can be globally pieced together using local information.
elements.sent-2411-013: A part of the BSD conjecture also includes a conjecture about the structure of $(E/\mathbb{Q )$: \begin{equation}|(E/Q)| \propto \frac{L'(E, 1)}{\Omega(E)R(E)\prod_{p}c_p}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-014: His work offers key insights into the BSD conjecture, especially for $n \equiv 5, 6, 7 \pmod{8}$.
elements.sent-2411-015: For the case of $n \equiv 5, 6, 7 \pmod{8}$, the weak Goldfeld conjecture offers further refinement, suggesting that the probability that the rank of $E_n$ is 1 and that $L(E_n, 1) \neq 0$ dominates in these congruence classes.
elements.sent-2411-016: This indicates that such $n$ are more likely to be congruent numbers.
elements.sent-2411-017: Smith's work proves the main theorem under the framework of the weak Goldfeld conjecture: \begin{thm (Smith, \cite{Smith 2016 ) For all positive integers $n$ such that $n \equiv 5, 6, 7 \pmod{8}$, a partial form of the BSD conjecture holds for the elliptic curve $E_n$, namely, $rank(E(\mathbb{Q )) = 1$ and $L(E_n, 1) \neq 0$.. \end{thm.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-018: Additionally, we demonstrate that the $L $-series coefficient $a_m=0$ when $m\equiv3\pmod{4}$ in the third section.
elements.sent-2411-019: In the fourth section, we explain that $y^2=x^3-n^2 x$ over $\mathbb{C}$ is isomorphic to $\mathbb{C /\left(\frac{\pi {\sqrt{n G \mathbb{Z [i]\right)$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: In the fifth and sixth sections, we introduce the modularity of elliptic curves and Heegner points, and present a method for determining whether \( n \) is a congruence.
elements.sent-2411-021: In the seventh section, we use this method to prove that 13 is a congruence..
elements.sent-2411-022: The conductor $ N $ of $ E $ is defined by \begin{equation}N = \prod_{p|\Delta(E)} p^{e_p},\end{equation} where \begin{equation}e_p =\left\{ \begin{aligned} 1, \quadif E has multiplicative reduction modulo p; \geq 2, \quadif E has additive reduction modulo p . \end{aligned} \right.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}e_p =\left\{  1, \quadif E has multiplicative reduction modulo p; \geq 2, \quadif E has additive reduction modulo p .  \right.}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-023: According to the definition, we have \( N = \prod p^{i_p \), where \( i_p \) is related to the singularity type.}
elements.sent-2411-024: To find the singularity type of \( E_p: E \mod p \), we need to find its singular points: \begin{equation}\left\{ \begin{aligned} y^2 - (x^3 - n^2 x) \equiv 0 \pmod{p}, \frac{\partial}{\partial x} (y^2 - x^3 + n^2 x) \equiv 0 \pmod{p}, \frac{\partial}{\partial y} (y^2 - x^3 + n^2 x) \equiv 0 \pmod{p}; \end{aligned}\right. \Rightarrow \left\{\begin{aligned} y^2 - x^3 \equiv 0 \pmod{p}, -3x^2 \equiv 0 \pmod{p}, 2y \equiv 0 \pmod{p}. \end{aligned}\right.\end{equation}.}}
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}\left\{  y^2 - (x^3 - n^2 x) \equiv 0 \pmod{p}, \frac{\partial}{\partial x} (y^2 - x^3 + n^2 x) \equiv 0 \pmod{p}, \frac{\partial}{\partial y} (y^2 - x^3 + n^2 x) \equiv 0 \pmod{p}; \right. \Rightarrow \left\{ y^2 - x^3 \equiv 0 \pmod{p}, -3x^2 \equiv 0 \pmod{p}, 2y \equiv 0 \pmod{p}. \right.}}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-025: So, we get the singular point \((0,0)\).

elements.formula-2411-007: \begin{equation}H(x, y) =  \frac{\partial^2 F}{\partial x^2} \frac{\partial^2 F}{\partial x \partial y} \frac{\partial^2 F}{\partial y \partial x} \frac{\partial^2 F}{\partial y^2}  =  6x 0 0 2 .\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-028: Therefore, all singular points for \( E_p \) are cusps (additive reduction).

elements.section-2411-004: Calculation of the Period of an Elliptic Curve
elements.sent-2411-059: For elliptic curves over $\mathbb{C}$, they are isomorphic to $\mathbb{C /\Lambda$.}

elements.sent-2411-062: We need the following formulae to calculate the periods: \begin{prop (Knapp, \cite{Knapp1992 ) For $a < b < c$, there is \begin{equation}\int_a^b \frac{dx}{\sqrt{(x-a)(x-b)(x-c)}} = \frac{\pi}{M\left(\sqrt{c-a}, \sqrt{c-b}\right)},\end{equation} \begin{equation}\int_b^c \frac{dx}{\sqrt{(x-a)(x-b)(x-c)}} = \frac{i\pi}{M\left(\sqrt{c-a}, \sqrt{c-b}\right)},\end{equation} where $M(x, y)$ is the arithmetic-geometric mean of $x$ and $y$.}}
elements.sent-2411-063: Let $a=-n$, $b=0$, $c=n$ in Proposition 4.2, we can get \begin{equation}\omega_1 = \frac{\pi}{M\left(\sqrt{2n}, \sqrt{n}\right)} \quad \text{and} \quad \omega_2 = \frac{i\pi}{M\left(\sqrt{2n}, \sqrt{n}\right)}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-064: Since $M\left(\sqrt{2n , \sqrt{n}\right) = \sqrt{n M(\sqrt{2 , 1)$ and \begin{equation}1/M(\sqrt{2}, 1) = G \quad (Gauss's constant, approximately  0.8346268 \ldots ),\end{equation} we have \begin{equation}\omega_1 = \frac{\pi}{\sqrt{n} }G \quad \text{and} \quad \omega_2 = \frac{i \pi}{\sqrt{n} }G.\end{equation}. So \begin{equation}\Lambda = \mathbb{Z} \frac{\pi}{\sqrt{n} }G + i \mathbb{Z} \frac{\pi}{\sqrt{n} }G = \frac{\pi}{\sqrt{n} }G \mathbb{Z}[i].\end{equation}.}}}
